channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: With Possible Raúl Castro Indictment, U.S. Eyes Venezuela Playbook
item.description: Amid stepped-up surveillance flights, a visit of the C.I.A. director and an energy embargo, the White House is trying to increase pressure on Cuba.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes, Tyler Pager, Eric Schmitt, Michael Crowley and Frances Robles
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Raúl Castro is the former president of Cuba and younger brother of Fidel Castro.

item.title: Photos: Cuba Plunges Into Darkness as U.S. Cuts Off Oil Supply
item.description: The United States has choked off Cuba’s fuel supply, plunging the already impoverished island into an acute energy crisis.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:description: People playing dominoes by flashlight as a protest fire burns in the background in Havana on Thursday.

item.title: Trump’s ‘Learning Curve’ on China Ends With Conciliation at Summit
item.description: The president has shifted the foundations of American policy toward China, throwing aside the adversarial approach of recent years.
item.dc:creator: Anton Troianovski and David E. Sanger
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: President Trump and President Xi Jinping of China arriving for the state banquet in Beijing on Thursday.

item.title: Xi Pitches His Vision for Avoiding Conflict With the U.S.
item.description: Having fought the Trump administration to a draw, China’s Xi Jinping is proposing “constructive strategic stability,” aimed at drawing lines he thinks the U.S. should not cross.
item.dc:creator: David Pierson and Lily Kuo
item.media:description: President Xi Jinping of China welcomed President Trump in Beijing on Thursday.

item.title: For Xi’s Critics, Trump’s China Visit Is Fuel for Jokes They Can’t Tell
item.description: On Threads and other sites, liberal-minded Chinese accounts were mocking the proceedings and offering a rare window into opinions on Xi Jinping and his leadership style.
item.dc:creator: Li Yuan
item.media:description: President Trump was cheered by children upon his arrival in Beijing on Thursday

item.title: Kataib Hezbollah Commander Accused of Planning Attacks on N.Y.C.
item.description: Mohammad Baqer Saad Dawood Al-Saadi is accused of leading attacks in Europe and being part of a retaliation campaign by Iran. Prosecutors say he is a leader of Kataib Hezbollah, an Iraqi militia with ties to Iran.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Weiser and Olivia Bensimon
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Kataib Hezbollah: What to Know
item.description: A commander of one of the most-hard line and powerful Iranian proxies in Iraq has been charged with plotting to attack Jewish sites in the United States.
item.dc:creator: Pranav Baskar
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A protester holding a Kataib Hezbollah flag in Baghdad, Iraq, in March. The militia was founded after the 2003 U.S. invasion of Iraq,

item.title: Snorkeling at Pearl Harbor: Kash Patel’s Travels Add to Focus on Ethical Issues
item.description: The F.B.I. director continues to come under scrutiny for mixing government business with dates, vacation and leisure time.
item.dc:creator: Elizabeth Williamson and Adam Goldman
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: The memorial above the sunken battleship USS Arizona in Hawaii in 2016. Kash Patel, the F.B.I. director, was accompanied by Navy SEALs on a “V.I.P. Snorkel” over the site that holds the remains of 1,100 Navy sailors and Marines who died at Pearl Harbor.

item.title: Justice Dept. Aims to Use Terrorism Laws to Target Mexican Officials
item.description: In a new tactic, the Justice Department this week instructed federal prosecutors to build criminal drug cases against Mexican officials using terrorism statutes.
item.dc:creator: Ernesto Londoño, Alan Feuer and Jack Nicas
item.media:credit: Alejandro Cegarra for The New York Times
item.media:description: Flags at the U.S. border with Mexico in Tijuana last year.

item.title: Michigan Battles Trump Over His Order to Keep an Old Coal Plant Running
item.description: The Trump administration broke the law, Michigan and others told a court, by declaring an “energy emergency” and forcing an aging coal-burning plant to keep operating.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:credit: Jim West/UCG/Universal Images Group, via Getty Images
item.media:description: The J.H. Campbell coal-burning power plant in West Olive, Mich. The state is challenging a Trump order to keep the aging plant active.

item.title: Archaeologists Find Egyptian Mummy Buried With the ‘Iliad’
item.description: For the deceased of Roman-era Egypt, Greek literature may have offered a cheat code to a more comfortable afterlife.
item.dc:creator: Franz Lidz
item.media:credit: Maite Mascort i Roca and Esther Pons Mellado, Mission of the University of Barcelona at Oxyrhynchus
item.media:description: The necropolis of Oxyrhynchus in Egypt, where a nonroyal mummy was found with some lines on papyrus from Book 2 of Homer’s “Iliad.”

item.title: Fierce Fights Over House Seats to Replace Nancy Pelosi and Jerrold Nadler
item.description: Nancy Pelosi and Jerrold Nadler are retiring, prompting competitive races to succeed them. Whoever wins their seats could tap big donors and shape the Democratic Party.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Oreskes and Heather Knight
item.media:credit: Pete Marovich for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Jerry Nadler and Nancy Pelosi, then the House speaker, loomed large in their respective districts. Now that they are both retiring, a fierce fight is on to replace them.

item.title: Republicans Redrew His District. Now This Democratic Stalwart Is Out.
item.description: Representative Steve Cohen has represented Memphis since 2007. After Republicans redistricted his seat, he is leaving the field, possibly to his young rival, Justin J. Pearson.
item.dc:creator: Emily Cochrane
item.media:description: Representative Steve Cohen, Democrat of Tennessee, held a news conference in his office on Capitol Hill on Friday to announce his retirement.

item.title: Texas Supreme Court Rejects Abbott Effort to Remove Democrats After Walkout
item.description: Republican state leaders tried to expel Democratic lawmakers during their fight over redistricting last year. The court said it was not necessary.
item.dc:creator: J. David Goodman
item.media:credit: Jim Vondruska for The New York Times
item.media:description: The top Democrat in the Texas House, Gene Wu, was among the lawmakers Gov. Greg Abbott sought to expel last year.

item.title: Tina Peters, Colorado Election Denier, Will Be Freed by Gov. Jared Polis
item.description: Gov. Jared Polis, a Democrat, commuted the sentence of Ms. Peters, a former county clerk serving a nine-year sentence for her role in a plot to examine voting machines after the 2020 election.
item.dc:creator: Jack Healy and Nick Corasaniti
item.media:credit: Daniel Brenner for The New York Times
item.media:description: Tina Peters, the former clerk of Mesa County, Colo., was sentenced to nine years in prison in 2024 after being found guilty of tampering with voting machines.

item.title: Trump Loyalist Leads Administration’s Hunt for Fraud in Elections
item.description: The loyalist, Dan Bishop, put his fealty to the president on display when he pressed the F.B.I. to chase investigative leads it had already rejected.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer and Nick Corasaniti
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times
item.media:description: During his time in the House, from 2019 to 2025, Mr. Bishop served on the subcommittee on the weaponization of the federal government.

item.title: Martin Short and the Secret to Finding Joy While Surviving Tragedy
item.description: The comedy star, who is the subject of a new documentary, has faced a series of unimaginable losses. Yet he says there are always laughs to remember.
item.dc:creator: Jason Zinoman and Thea Traff
item.media:description: Grieving gives you “muscles to survive,” Martin Short said.

item.title: The Strange Alliance Trying to Remake American Psychiatry
item.description: The Department of Health and critical psychiatry activists are hoping to upend 40 years of medical orthodoxy.
item.dc:creator: Daniel Bergner

item.title: Falling Chunks of Debris Are Endangering Drivers on a Busy N.Y.C. Road
item.description: After two incidents, crews made repairs and installed protective netting on the Trans-Manhattan Expressway, which leads to the George Washington Bridge.
item.dc:creator: Patrick McGeehan
item.category: Port Authority of New York and New Jersey

item.title: Replacing a Royal Hand-Me-Down That Finally Frayed
item.description: Three years after King Charles III was crowned, the Royal Opera House in London hung plush new stage curtains bearing the monarch’s insignia — but only after the old ones gave up the ghost.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.category: Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
item.media:credit: Jeremie Souteyrat for The New York Times

item.title: In Italy, Princess Catherine Puts Her Wardrobe to Work
item.description: Suits her, sir.
item.dc:creator: Vanessa Friedman
item.media:description: Executive arrival: Princess Catherine greeting the crowd at the city hall in Reggio Emilia in a pantsuit by the London designer Edeline Lee.

item.title: Why ‘Smart’ Products Have Started to Look Like the Dumb Choice
item.description: How Wi-Fi-connecting, app-based tech led to a backlash in the name of simplicity.
item.dc:creator: Nitsuh Abebe

item.title: The Damage of ‘Trump Math’ Is Adding Up
item.description: Authoritarians go after data. The president has already started.
item.dc:creator: Binyamin Appelbaum and James Robinson

item.title: Keir Starmer and the End of Britain’s Old Certainties
item.description: The decade since Brexit has been one long, painful process of trying — and failing — to make sense of this new reality.
item.dc:creator: Tom McTague

item.title: NPR Podcast Host Ramtin Arablouei Exits Amid Workplace Inquiry
item.description: Ramtin Arablouei, a co-host of “Throughline,” left the network after an employee made a human resources complaint about his behavior.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin
item.media:credit: Jason Andrew for The New York Times
item.media:description: NPR studios in Washington, D.C.

item.title: Stock Market Rally Confronts Inflation and Higher Interest Rates
item.description: Despite accelerating inflation and possible interest rate increases, the S&P 500 has posted a long weekly winning streak, driven by strong corporate earnings. Can it last?
item.dc:creator: Joe Rennison

item.title: Five Italians Die While Scuba Diving Deep Caves in the Maldives
item.description: The divers, including a marine scientist and her daughter, were part of a research trip and were exploring an underwater cave system when they failed to resurface.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel and Josephine de La Bruyère
item.media:description: Divers and guides returning to shore in Malé, capital of the Maldives, a popular diving destination. The disappearance of five divers this week set off a large search mission, but the authorities have recovered only one body.

item.title: Cornell Criticizes Students After Its President Bumps Them With His Car
item.description: After the president bumped into students with his car, an investigation by the university’s trustees said that the students’ actions were “inconsistent with university policies.”
item.dc:creator: Santul Nerkar
item.media:description: An investigation by Cornell University into an episode in which the school’s president bumped into students with his vehicle effectively absolved him of blame.
